why: Sensitive to outliers; most common measure of central tendency but not always representative.

q: What is the median?
a: The middle value when data is ordered; less affected by outliers
why: For even-sized sets, median is the average of the two middle values.

q: What is the mode?
a: The value appearing most frequently in a dataset
why: Useful for categorical data; a dataset can have multiple modes or no mode.

q: What is standard deviation?
a: A measure of spread around the mean
why: Quantifies how far typical values deviate from the average; larger std dev means more variability.

q: What is variance?
a: The square of standard deviation; average squared deviation from mean
why: Variance is mathematically convenient for calculations; standard deviation is the square root.

q: What is a normal distribution?
a: A bell-shaped probability distribution, symmetric around the mean
why: Very common in nature; described by mean and standard deviation; 68% of data falls within 1 std dev.

q: What is the law of large numbers?
a: As samples increase, the average approaches the true expected value
why: Justifies why larger surveys are more reliable than small polls.

q: What is conditional probability?
a: The probability of an event given that another event has occurred
why: Notation P(A|B) reads 'probability of A given B'; allows reasoning about dependent events.

q: What is a sample space?
a: The set of all possible outcomes in a probability experiment
why: Defines the universe for a problem; all event probabilities must lie within it.

q: What is Zeno's paradox?
a: Apparent logical proof that motion is impossible through infinite subdivision
why: Resolved by calculus: infinite series can sum to finite values, so motion isn't halted by infinity.

q: What is the pigeonhole principle?
a: If n+1 items go into n boxes, at least one box has two items
why: Simple but powerful for proving existence results in combinatorics and logic.

q: What is proof by contradiction?
a: Assume the opposite, derive a contradiction, conclude the original statement was true.
why: Common technique for difficult problems; shows impossibility elegantly.

q: What is mathematical induction?
a: Prove a statement for base case, then for n+1 if true for n
why: Standard method for proving formulas work for all positive integers.

q: What is the fundamental theorem of calculus?
a: Differentiation and integration are inverse operations
why: Ties geometry (area) to algebra (slopes); unifies the two major branches of calculus.

q: What is a derivative?
a: The rate of change of a function; slope of the tangent line
why: Captures instantaneous rate; derivative of position is velocity, derivative of velocity is acceleration.

q: What is an integral?
a: The antiderivative of a function; accumulation or area under a curve
why: Inverse of derivative; can compute areas, volumes, and total quantities from rates.

q: What is a limit in calculus?
a: The value a function approaches as input approaches some value
why: Defines calculus rigorously; enables us to talk about values at undefined points.

q: What does 'continuous' mean for a function?
a: No breaks or jumps in the graph; limit equals function value at every point
why: Continuous functions have nice properties like intermediate value theorem and integrability.

q: What is a function?
a: A relation where each input has exactly one output
why: Foundation of mathematics; notation f(x) means 'output of f when input is x'.

q: What is the slope of a line?
a: Rise over run (vertical change divided by horizontal change)
why: Constant for linear functions; describes steepness and direction.

q: What is acceleration in calculus terms?
a: The second derivative of position with respect to time
why: First derivative gives velocity, second gives acceleration—tells how fast velocity is changing.

q: What is a logical statement?
a: A sentence that is either true or false, not both
why: Foundation of logic; allows us to reason formally about propositions.

q: What is a tautology?
a: A logical statement that is always true regardless of inputs
why: Example: A or not-A is always true; tautologies are logical truths.

q: What is a contradiction?
a: A logical statement that is always false
why: Example: A and not-A is always false; contradictions reveal logical impossibility.

q: What is a set?
a: A collection of distinct objects
why: Sets are abstract containers; elements either belong or don't—no duplicates or order.

q: What is an element of a set?
a: An object that belongs to the set
why: Notation a ∈ S means 'a is an element of set S'.

q: What is the union of two sets?
a: The set of all elements in either set
why: Notation A ∪ B; includes everything from both, no duplicates.

q: What is the intersection of two sets?
a: The set of elements common to both sets
why: Notation A ∩ B; contains only elements in both sets.

q: What is a subset?
a: A set where every element is also in another set
why: A ⊆ B means all elements of A are in B; allows partial containment.

q: Who was Euclid?
a: Ancient Greek mathematician who systematized geometry
why: His Elements was the authoritative math text for 2000 years; founded axiomatic method.

q: Who was Pythagoras?
a: Ancient Greek mathematician and philosopher
why: Pythagoras' theorem (though known earlier) bears his name; founded a mystical school around numbers.

q: Who was Isaac Newton?
a: English mathematician and physicist who developed calculus
why: Co-invented calculus with Leibniz; revolutionized physics and mathematics in the 17th century.

q: Who was Leonhard Euler?
a: Swiss mathematician with enormous output across all fields
why: Most prolific mathematician; introduced modern notation and solved problems in almost every area.

q: Who was Carl Friedrich Gauss?
a: German mathematician called 'Prince of Mathematicians'
why: Contributed to number theory, statistics, algebra, analysis; normal distribution named after him.

q: What is compound interest?
a: Interest earned on principal plus previously earned interest
why: Formula A = P(1 + r/n)^(nt); exponential growth makes it powerful for long-term investments.

q: What is simple interest?
a: Interest calculated only on the original principal, not accumulated interest
why: Formula: I = P × r × t; grows linearly instead of exponentially.

q: What is a percentage?
a: A fraction expressed as a number out of 100
why: Makes proportions intuitive; 50% means 50/100 or half.

q: What is the difference between mean and median?
a: Mean is average; median is middle value when sorted
why: Mean includes all values so it's affected by outliers; median is robust to extreme values.

q: What is depreciation?
a: Decrease in value of an asset over time
why: Used in accounting and economics; depreciation rate tells how quickly value declines.

q: What is an exponent?
a: A number indicating how many times to multiply a base by itself
why: a^n means a multiplied by itself n times; rules like a^m × a^n = a^(m+n) simplify calculations.

q: What is a logarithm?
a: The inverse of exponentiation; log_b(x) = y means b^y = x
why: Converts multiplication to addition; logarithms compress large numbers and appear in many natural processes.

q: What is the natural logarithm?
a: Logarithm with base e, written ln(x)
why: Appears in calculus and continuous growth; inverse of e^x.

q: What is absolute value?
a: The distance from a number to zero, always non-negative
why: Written |x|; removes sign information, useful for measuring magnitude.

q: What is a fraction?
a: A ratio of two integers; numerator divided by denominator
why: Represents parts of a whole; can always be expressed with a common denominator for comparison.

q: What does it mean to simplify a fraction?
a: Reduce to lowest terms by dividing numerator and denominator by GCD
why: 8/12 and 2/3 are equal; simplification reveals the simplest form.

q: What is an asymptote?
a: A line that a curve approaches but never reaches
why: Horizontal asymptotes describe long-term behavior; vertical asymptotes indicate undefined points.

q: What is a matrix?
a: A rectangular array of numbers arranged in rows and columns
why: Matrices enable compact representation of systems of equations and transformations.

q: What is matrix multiplication?
a: Combining matrices where element (i,j) is dot product of row i and column j
why: Not commutative (A×B ≠ B×A); order matters, used in computer graphics and linear systems.

q: What is a determinant of a matrix?
a: A scalar value derived from a square matrix; indicates invertibility
why: det(A) = 0 means matrix is singular (non-invertible); relates to area/volume scaling.

q: What is an eigenvalue?
a: A scalar such that matrix times vector equals scalar times vector
why: Eigenvectors are special directions where matrix acts as simple scaling; used in stability analysis.

q: What is a sequence?
a: An ordered list of numbers following a pattern or rule
why: Arithmetic and geometric sequences are most common; limits of sequences underpin calculus.

q: What is the Fibonacci sequence?
a: 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8... where each term is sum of previous two
why: Appears in nature (spirals, flowers); ratio of consecutive terms approaches the golden ratio.

q: What is a permutation?
a: An arrangement of objects where order matters
why: n! (n factorial) counts permutations of n objects; distinguishes from combinations where order doesn't matter.

q: What is a transistor?
a: A tiny electronic switch that can amplify or switch electronic signals.
why: Transistors are the fundamental building blocks of all modern computers; billions are etched onto a single chip to form processors.

q: What does CPU stand for?
a: Central Processing Unit.
why: The CPU executes instructions and performs calculations, making it the brain of the computer.

q: How many bits are in a byte?
a: Eight bits.
why: A byte is the standard unit of computer memory and storage; one byte can represent 256 different values (0-255).

q: What is RAM?
a: Random Access Memory; fast, volatile memory used while programs run.
why: RAM is much faster than disk storage but loses all data when powered off, unlike permanent storage.

q: What is a cache?
a: Ultra-fast memory close to CPU that stores frequently accessed data.
why: Cache dramatically reduces the time CPU spends waiting for data from slower main memory.

q: What does GPU stand for?
a: Graphics Processing Unit.
why: GPUs excel at parallel processing and are now used for graphics, AI training, and other compute-intensive tasks.

q: What is machine code?
a: Instructions written in binary that the CPU can directly execute.
why: All high-level programming languages ultimately compile down to machine code for the processor to run.

q: What are logic gates?
a: Electronic circuits that perform boolean operations (AND, OR, NOT) on binary inputs.
why: Logic gates are building blocks that combine to form all digital circuits in computers.

q: What is an instruction set architecture?
a: The set of machine code instructions a processor can execute.
why: Different processors (x86, ARM) have different ISAs, which is why software must be compiled for specific architectures.

q: What does Moore's Law describe?
a: The observation that transistor density on chips doubles roughly every two years.
why: Moore's Law has driven exponential growth in computing power over decades, though it is slowing down now.

q: What is a variable?
a: Named container that stores a value or data that can change during program execution.
why: Variables let programmers refer to data by meaningful names instead of memory addresses.

q: What is a 'pure function' in programming?
a: A function whose output depends only on its inputs, with no side effects.
why: Pure functions are easier to test and reason about because they never alter state outside themselves.

q: What is an object in Object-Oriented Programming?
a: A self-contained entity combining data (properties) and behavior (methods).
why: Objects model real-world entities and allow programmers to organize code into logical, reusable components.

q: What is inheritance in OOP?
a: Mechanism where a class inherits properties and methods from a parent class.
why: Inheritance promotes code reuse and creates hierarchical relationships between types.

q: What is a loop?
a: Control structure that repeats a block of code while a condition is true.
why: Loops let programmers process collections or repeat tasks without duplicating code.

q: What is recursion?
a: A function calling itself with a simpler input until reaching a base case.
why: Recursion is elegant for problems that naturally break into similar subproblems, like tree traversal.

q: What is a conditional statement?
a: Code that executes different blocks based on whether a condition is true or false.
why: Conditionals let programs make decisions and branch into different execution paths.

q: What is an API?
a: Application Programming Interface; defined way for software components to communicate.
why: APIs abstract complexity and let different software systems interact without knowing internal implementation details.

q: What is a library or framework?
a: Pre-written code that provides reusable functions or structure for common programming tasks.
why: Libraries and frameworks save developers time by providing tested, optimized solutions instead of reinventing the wheel.

q: What is a debugger?
a: Development tool that lets programmers step through code and inspect variables to find bugs.
why: Debuggers are essential for understanding why programs behave unexpectedly and fixing errors efficiently.

q: What is polymorphism?
a: Ability of code to work with objects of different types in a uniform way.
why: Polymorphism enables flexible, extensible code where new types can be added without changing existing logic.

q: What is encapsulation?
a: Bundling data and methods together and hiding internal details from outside access.
why: Encapsulation protects data integrity and lets implementations change internally without breaking external code.

q: What is an exception?
a: An error or unexpected condition that disrupts normal program flow.
why: Exception handling lets programs gracefully respond to errors instead of crashing.

q: What is type casting?
a: Converting a variable from one data type to another.
why: Type casting bridges incompatible types but must be done carefully to avoid data loss or errors.

q: What is garbage collection?
a: Automatic memory management that frees memory no longer in use by the program.
why: Garbage collection prevents memory leaks and simplifies programming compared to manual memory management.

q: What does Big-O notation describe?
a: How an algorithm's runtime or space grows as input size increases.
why: Big-O helps compare algorithm efficiency; O(n) means linear growth, O(n squared) means quadratic.

q: What is linear search?
a: Algorithm that checks each element sequentially until finding the target or reaching the end.
why: Linear search is simple but slow (O(n)) for large datasets; works on unsorted data.

q: What is binary search?
a: Efficient algorithm that repeatedly divides sorted data in half to find target.
why: Binary search is much faster (O(log n)) than linear search but requires data to be sorted first.

q: What is a sorting algorithm?
a: Algorithm that arranges elements in a specific order, typically ascending or descending.
why: Sorting is fundamental; fast sorts like quicksort (O(n log n)) are far more efficient than naive bubble sort.

q: What is a hash function?
a: Function that converts input of any size into fixed-size output (hash), typically for lookups.
why: Hash functions enable O(1) average lookup time in hash tables; used in databases, caching, cryptography.

q: What is a greedy algorithm?
a: Algorithm that makes locally optimal choice at each step, hoping for global optimum.
why: Greedy algorithms are fast but do not always find the best solution; useful for approximation and specific problems.

q: What is dynamic programming?
a: Optimization technique that solves complex problems by breaking into overlapping subproblems.
why: Dynamic programming caches subproblem results to avoid recomputation; trades space for time efficiency.

q: What is a divide-and-conquer algorithm?
a: Approach that breaks problem into smaller subproblems, solves them independently, combines results.
why: Divide-and-conquer enables efficient algorithms like merge sort and enables parallelization.

q: What is time complexity?
a: Measure of how runtime of an algorithm grows with input size.
why: Time complexity lets programmers estimate if an algorithm will be fast enough for large datasets.

q: What is space complexity?
a: Measure of how much memory an algorithm uses as input size grows.
why: Space complexity is critical for systems with limited memory; sometimes trading time for space is worth it.

q: What is an array?
a: Ordered collection of elements of the same type stored in contiguous memory.
why: Arrays provide fast random access (O(1)) but slow insertion and deletion (O(n)).

q: What is a linked list?
a: Data structure where elements are stored in nodes with pointers to next node.
why: Linked lists allow efficient insertion and deletion (O(1)) but slow random access (O(n)).

q: What is a tree?
a: Hierarchical data structure with a root node and child nodes arranged in parent-child relationships.
why: Trees efficiently model hierarchies and enable fast searching; binary search trees search in O(log n).

q: What is a binary tree?
a: Tree where each node has at most two children, typically left and right.
why: Binary trees are simple, efficient, and form the basis for many algorithms and data structures.

q: What is a graph?
a: Data structure with nodes (vertices) and edges connecting them, with optional direction and weights.
why: Graphs model complex relationships like social networks, maps, and dependencies.

q: What is a queue?
a: Data structure following First-In-First-Out (FIFO) principle; add at back, remove from front.
why: Queues model real-world scenarios like printer queues and are used in breadth-first search.

q: What is a stack?
a: Data structure following Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) principle; add and remove from same end.
why: Stacks power function call mechanisms and enable undo and redo; used in depth-first search.

q: What is a hash table?
a: Data structure using hash function to map keys to array indices for fast lookups.
why: Hash tables provide O(1) average case lookup, insertion, deletion; form the basis of dictionaries and maps.

q: What is a heap?
a: Specialized tree-based structure where parent nodes have priority over children.
why: Heaps efficiently support priority queue operations and enable heap sort algorithm.

q: What is a set data structure?
a: Collection that stores unique elements with no particular order.
why: Sets efficiently test membership and remove duplicates; implemented via hash tables or trees.

q: What does TCP/IP stand for?
a: Transmission Control Protocol and Internet Protocol; fundamental suite for internet communication.
why: TCP/IP defines how data is packaged, routed, and reassembled across networks; governs the entire internet.

q: What is HTTP?
a: HyperText Transfer Protocol; protocol for transferring web pages and resources.
why: HTTP is stateless and request-response based; HTTPS adds encryption for security.

q: What is HTTPS?
a: HTTP with TLS or SSL encryption layer to secure data in transit.
why: HTTPS prevents eavesdropping and tampering; now standard for all sensitive web communication.

q: What is DNS?
a: Domain Name System; translates human-readable domain names to IP addresses.
why: DNS is like the internet's phonebook; without it, you would need to memorize IP addresses.

q: What is an IP address?
a: Unique numerical identifier assigned to devices on a network.
why: IP addresses route data packets; IPv4 uses 32 bits (4.3 billion addresses), IPv6 uses 128 bits.

q: What is a port in networking?
a: Virtual endpoint on a device for a specific service or protocol.
why: Ports let a single device run multiple services; web typically uses port 80 (HTTP) or 443 (HTTPS).

q: What is a firewall?
a: Network security system that monitors and controls incoming and outgoing network traffic.
why: Firewalls block unauthorized access and malicious traffic based on rules.

q: What is bandwidth?
a: Maximum rate at which data can be transmitted over a connection.
why: Bandwidth limits how much data flows per second; measured in bits per second (bps).

q: What is latency?
a: Time it takes for data to travel from source to destination.
why: Low latency is critical for real-time applications like gaming and video calls.

q: What is a packet?
a: Small unit of data with headers for routing and reassembly.
why: Networks break data into packets for efficient transmission and routing.

q: What is routing?
a: Process of forwarding packets from source to destination across networks.
why: Routers use routing tables and algorithms to determine the best path for packets.

q: What is an IP subnet?
a: Logical subdivision of an IP network sharing a common address prefix.
why: Subnetting allows efficient organization and management of large networks.

q: What is binary?
a: Base-2 number system using only digits 0 and 1.
why: Binary is fundamental to computers because transistors are on/off switches naturally mapped to 0 and 1.

q: What is hexadecimal?
a: Base-16 number system using digits 0-9 and letters A-F.
why: Hex is compact and maps cleanly to binary (4 bits per hex digit), making it common in computing.

q: How do you convert decimal 10 to binary?
a: 1010 (eight plus two).
why: Binary conversion divides by 2 repeatedly, collecting remainders; important for understanding data representation.

q: What is a bit?
a: Smallest unit of data in computing; a single 0 or 1.
why: Bits are the foundation of all digital information; eight bits form a byte.

q: What is the difference between bits and bytes?
a: A byte is 8 bits; bit is singular unit, byte is standard memory unit.
why: Using bytes instead of bits simplifies memory addressing and human communication.

q: What is machine learning?
a: Field where systems learn patterns from data without explicit programming.
why: ML powers recommendations, image recognition, language models; enables computers to improve with experience.

q: What is a neural network?
a: Computational model inspired by biological neurons with interconnected layers.
why: Neural networks excel at pattern recognition; deep networks underpin modern AI breakthroughs.

q: What is training in machine learning?
a: Process of adjusting model parameters using data to minimize prediction errors.
why: Training is iterative; models learn by comparing predictions to actual results and updating weights.

q: What is overfitting?
a: When model learns training data too well, memorizing noise instead of generalizing.
why: Overfitting reduces performance on new data; addressed by regularization, early stopping, or more data.

q: What is supervised learning?
a: ML approach where model learns from labeled data (input-output pairs).
why: Supervised learning is common for classification and regression where ground truth labels exist.

q: What is unsupervised learning?
a: ML approach where model finds patterns in unlabeled data.
why: Unsupervised learning enables discovery of hidden structures; includes clustering and dimensionality reduction.

q: What is a dataset?
a: Collection of data used to train and test machine learning models.
why: Dataset quality and size directly impact model performance; garbage in, garbage out.

q: What is accuracy in machine learning?
a: Proportion of predictions that are correct out of total predictions.
why: Accuracy is intuitive but can be misleading if classes are imbalanced; precision and recall often matter more.

q: What is encryption?
a: Process of converting plaintext into unreadable ciphertext using a key.
why: Encryption protects data confidentiality; only someone with the key can decrypt.

q: What is a password hash?
a: One-way function converting password into fixed-length hash for secure storage.
why: Hashing passwords means servers never store actual passwords; even if database leaks, passwords stay safe.

q: What is a vulnerability?
a: Weakness in software or system that can be exploited to cause harm.
why: Vulnerabilities range from minor to critical; security researchers responsibly disclose them.

q: What is SQL injection?
a: Attack inserting malicious SQL code into input fields to manipulate database.
why: SQL injection is common and dangerous; prevented using parameterized queries and input validation.

q: What is a brute force attack?
a: Attack trying many password combinations until finding the correct one.
why: Brute force is simple but slow; mitigated by strong passwords and rate limiting.

q: What is two-factor authentication?
a: Security requiring two verification methods, typically password plus phone code or app.
why: Two-factor authentication significantly improves security by requiring something you know plus something you have.

q: What is a VPN?
a: Virtual Private Network; encrypts internet traffic and masks IP address for privacy.
why: VPNs hide your location and protect data from ISP or network sniffing.

q: What is a certificate in cryptography?
a: Digital file proving identity and enabling encrypted communication verification.
why: SSL and TLS certificates secure websites; browsers verify certificates to prevent man-in-the-middle attacks.

q: Who is Alan Turing?
a: Pioneering computer scientist; founder of theoretical computer science and Turing Machine.
why: Turing's work defined what computation is; Turing Test explores machine intelligence.

q: Who is Ada Lovelace?
a: First programmer; wrote first algorithm intended for machine execution in 1840s.
why: Lovelace saw beyond mechanical calculation to general computation; her notes on Babbage's Engine are brilliant.

q: Who is John von Neumann?
a: Mathematician and physicist; developed von Neumann architecture used in modern computers.
why: Von Neumann architecture separates memory from CPU; nearly all computers follow this design today.

q: What is the Turing Machine?
a: Abstract computational model consisting of infinite tape and read and write head.
why: Turing Machine defines computability; any algorithm implementable on one language can run on any Turing-complete system.

q: What is the von Neumann architecture?
a: Design with CPU, memory, input and output, and control units separated.
why: Von Neumann architecture enables general-purpose computing; still dominates despite alternatives like Harvard architecture.

q: What was ENIAC?
a: First general-purpose electronic computer, built 1945; weighed 30 tons, used 18,000 tubes.
why: ENIAC demonstrated electronic computing; though massive and slow, it proved automation of complex calculations.

q: What was the first programming language?
a: Plankalkul, designed by Konrad Zuse in 1940s; Fortran became first widely-used.
why: Early languages were assembly-like; each generation abstracted further from machine code.

q: When did the internet become publicly accessible?
a: 1990s, particularly after World Wide Web developed by Tim Berners-Lee in 1989.
why: Internet existed militarily since 1960s and 1970s; commercialization and web made it mainstream.

q: Who invented the transistor?
a: John Bardeen, Walter Brattain, and William Shockley at Bell Labs, 1947.
why: Bardeen won a second Nobel for superconductivity (BCS theory) — a different achievement.

q: Who is Grace Hopper?
a: Pioneering computer scientist; developed first compiler and influenced COBOL language design.
why: Hopper's compiler work showed machines could translate human-readable code to machine code automatically.

q: What is the Church-Turing thesis?
a: Hypothesis that effectively computable problems are those computable by Turing Machines.
why: This thesis bridges formal theory and practical computation; defines limits of what is algorithmically solvable.

q: What does FLOPS measure?
a: Floating Point Operations Per Second; measure of computational speed.
why: FLOPS helps compare processor power; supercomputers are rated in teraFLOPS or petaFLOPS.

q: What are the three main layers of Earth by density and composition?
a: Crust, mantle, core (outer and inner).
why: The crust is solid rock, mantle is hot dense rock that flows slowly, and the core is iron/nickel—the composition and density determine Earth's internal heat and magnetic field.

q: How thick is Earth's crust compared to Earth's radius?
a: Very thin: ~30-70 km vs 6,371 km radius.
why: The crust's thinness relative to radius (like eggshell on an egg) is why plate tectonics and volcanic activity affect the surface so dramatically.

q: What is the lithosphere and how does it differ from the crust?
a: Lithosphere is rigid crust + upper mantle; asthenosphere below is plastic/viscous.
why: The lithosphere plates float on the weaker asthenosphere, enabling plate motion that drives tectonics.

q: Explain plate tectonics theory in one sentence.
a: Earth's surface is divided into rigid plates that move, collide, and slide.
why: This motion explains earthquakes, volcanoes, mountain formation, and ocean spreading over geological time.

q: What happens at a divergent plate boundary?
a: Plates move apart, new oceanic crust forms, magma rises.
why: This is how the seafloor spreads and ocean basins grow, creating mid-ocean ridges.

q: What happens at a convergent plate boundary?
a: Plates collide; one may subduct (dive under), creating mountains or trenches.
why: Subduction zones generate the deepest earthquakes, volcanic arcs, and the tallest mountain ranges.

q: What happens at a transform plate boundary?
a: Plates slide past each other horizontally.
why: This motion causes large, shallow earthquakes (e.g., San Andreas Fault) without adding or removing crust.

q: What is the Richter scale and what does it measure?
a: Logarithmic scale measuring earthquake magnitude (energy released).
why: Each step (5 to 6) represents ~30× more energy, so scale is logarithmic not linear.

q: What is the difference between earthquake magnitude and intensity?
a: Magnitude is energy released (one number); intensity is damage observed (varies by location).
why: A magnitude-7 earthquake in a city is catastrophic; same magnitude in remote area causes little damage.

q: Why do most earthquakes occur at plate boundaries?
a: Stress builds as plates interact; energy is released suddenly as rupture.
why: Plate boundaries concentrate stress over geological time, making them earthquake hotspots.

q: What is a volcano and what causes volcanic eruptions?
a: A vent where magma erupts; pressure from hot gas and magma drives eruptions.
why: Magma is less dense than surrounding rock, so it rises; dissolved gases expand, causing explosive force.

q: Name three types of volcanoes and describe their shapes.
a: Shield (broad, gentle slopes), composite (steep, layered), cinder cone (small, steep).
why: Shape reflects eruption style: shield volcanoes erupt easily (low viscosity), composite volcanoes are explosive (high viscosity).

q: What is lava and how does it differ from magma?
a: Magma is molten rock underground; lava is magma that erupts at surface.
why: The same molten rock has different names depending on location—terminology distinction geologists use to clarify context.

q: What is a tsunami and how is it generated?
a: Large ocean wave caused by underwater earthquakes, landslides, or volcanoes.
why: Earthquakes displace water column vertically; the resulting waves travel at 500+ mph across open ocean.

q: Why are tsunami waves so destructive near shore?
a: Deep-ocean waves slow and compress as water depth decreases, building height.
why: Wave energy is conserved, so slower wave speed means taller wave—amplification from ocean floor to shore.

q: What is weathering and name two types.
a: Breakdown of rocks; mechanical (physical fracture) and chemical (mineral alteration).
why: Weathering by wind, water, ice, or acids gradually transforms solid rock into smaller fragments and soil.

q: What is erosion and how does it differ from weathering?
a: Erosion is transport of weathered material; weathering is in-place breakdown.
why: Weathering weakens rock; erosion removes it—both are needed to shape landscapes.

q: What are the three main types of rocks?
a: Igneous (formed from magma), sedimentary (from compacted sediment), metamorphic (from heat/pressure).
why: Rock cycle processes transform any rock type into another over geological timescales.

q: What is a mineral and name three common examples.
a: Naturally occurring, inorganic, crystalline solid; examples: quartz, feldspar, mica.
why: Minerals are the building blocks of rocks; their crystal structure and composition define properties like hardness and cleavage.

q: What is the Mohs hardness scale?
a: Scale 1-10 ranking mineral hardness; diamond is 10, talc is 1.
why: Hardness reflects bond strength in the crystal structure—harder minerals resist scratching because atoms are tightly bonded.

q: How are igneous rocks classified?
a: By cooling rate and mineral size: intrusive (slow, coarse) vs extrusive (fast, fine).
why: Granite cools slowly underground so crystals grow large; basalt cools fast at surface so crystals are tiny.

q: What is sedimentary rock and how does it form?
a: Rock formed from compaction and cementation of sediment (sand, silt, clay).
why: Sediment layers compress under overlying weight; minerals precipitate and bind grains together over time.

q: What is metamorphic rock and what triggers metamorphism?
a: Rock altered by heat, pressure, or both without melting; occurs at depth or collision zones.
why: Heat and pressure rearrange mineral crystals without crossing the melting point, creating new mineral assemblages.

q: What is the water cycle and name four main stages.
a: Evaporation, condensation, precipitation, collection (or infiltration, runoff).
why: Solar energy drives evaporation; cooling causes condensation; gravity pulls water down as precipitation—cycle is continuous.

q: What is the difference between weather and climate?
a: Weather is day-to-day conditions; climate is long-term patterns (decades+).
why: Weather is chaotic short-term; climate emerges from averaging weather over long timescales and reflects energy balance.

q: What causes seasons on Earth?
a: Axial tilt (~23.5°) toward/away from Sun as Earth orbits.
why: Northern/southern hemispheres receive different solar intensity at different times of year due to tilt angle.

q: What is the greenhouse effect and name two greenhouse gases.
a: Atmospheric gases trap heat; examples: CO2, methane, water vapor.
why: Greenhouse gases absorb infrared radiation that would otherwise escape, warming the surface and lower atmosphere.

q: How has atmospheric CO2 changed over the last 200 years?
a: Increased from ~280 to ~420 ppm due to fossil fuel burning.
why: Ice core records show pre-industrial stability; rapid rise correlates with industrialization and is confirmed by multiple datasets.

q: What is the structure of Earth's atmosphere and name three layers.
a: Troposphere (weather), stratosphere (ozone), mesosphere, thermosphere, exosphere.
why: Temperature increases/decreases with altitude in each layer depending on gas composition and solar absorption.

q: What is the ozone layer and why is it important?
a: Ozone (O3) in stratosphere absorbs UV-B radiation, protecting life.
why: Without it, UV-B would damage DNA and harm organisms; CFCs threaten it by releasing chlorine that destroys ozone.

q: What is the difference between weather fronts?
a: Cold front: cold air mass advances; warm front: warm air mass advances.
why: Cold fronts are steeper and produce intense storms; warm fronts are gradual and bring milder weather.

q: What causes wind?
a: Pressure differences from uneven solar heating.
why: Hot air rises and flows toward cooler areas, creating pressure gradients that drive wind.

q: What is the Coriolis effect and how does it affect wind?
a: Earth's rotation deflects moving air/water to the right (Northern Hemisphere).
why: This deflection explains why hurricanes rotate and why trade winds blow from northeast/southeast rather than directly north/south.

q: What are jet streams and what role do they play?
a: Fast-moving narrow air currents in upper troposphere; steer weather systems.
why: Jet streams form at boundaries between air masses of different temperatures; they guide storm tracks and influence regional climate.

q: What is a hurricane and what conditions create them?
a: Tropical cyclone with sustained winds >74 mph; needs warm ocean water (>26.5°C).
why: Warm water evaporates, rising air creates low pressure, Coriolis rotation strengthens spinning; cooler water or land weakens the system.

q: Why are tropical cyclones called by different names?
a: Regional convention: hurricanes (Atlantic/E. Pacific), typhoons (W. Pacific), cyclones (Indian Ocean).
why: Same meteorological phenomenon; different names reflect regional weather terminology, not structural differences.

q: What is a tornado and how does it form?
a: Violently rotating column of air; forms from supercell thunderstorms under wind shear.
why: Strong wind shear tilts updraft; rotation tightens under conservation of angular momentum, forming visible funnel.

q: What is the difference between climate and weather variability?
a: Climate variability: long-term oscillations (El Niño); weather variability: day-to-day randomness.
why: El Niño shifts ocean temperatures, affecting rainfall patterns for months; weather forecasts fail after 2 weeks due to chaos.

q: What is El Niño and what are its effects?
a: Warming of eastern Pacific Ocean; shifts rainfall, temperature patterns globally.
why: During El Niño, Pacific pressure flips, weakening trade winds and moving warm water eastward, disrupting normal weather.

q: What is the difference between El Niño and La Niña?
a: El Niño: warm Pacific; La Niña: cold Pacific; opposite effects on weather.
why: Both represent extremes of natural oscillation; La Niña intensifies some patterns El Niño suppresses.

q: What is ocean salinity and what causes it?
a: Salt concentration in seawater (~35 ppt); from dissolved minerals from rocks and volcanic outgassing.
why: Salt accumulates because only water evaporates, leaving dissolved minerals behind over billions of years.

q: How does ocean salinity affect water density and circulation?
a: Higher salinity increases density; drives thermohaline circulation (deep ocean currents).
why: Cold, salty water is dense and sinks, pulling warm surface water poleward—global 'conveyor belt' for heat transport.

q: What are ocean currents and name two types.
a: Surface currents (driven by wind) and deep currents (driven by density).
why: Surface currents are fast and warm; deep currents are slow and cold but carry more total heat globally.

q: What is the Gulf Stream and why is Europe warmer than expected?
a: Warm Atlantic current flowing northeast; brings tropical heat to high latitudes.
why: Northern Europe is 5-10°C warmer than its latitude suggests because Gulf Stream brings warm water and heat northward.

q: What is an ocean gyre and name one example.
a: Large circular ocean current system; example: North Atlantic Gyre.
why: Wind drives subtropical gyres to rotate clockwise (Northern Hemisphere), concentrating warm water in center.

q: What causes ocean tides?
a: Moon's gravity pulls water; Sun also contributes; tides are bulges on both sides of Earth.
why: Moon's gravity pulls nearer side more strongly, creating bulge there; far side bulge results from Earth being pulled more than water there.

q: Why are there usually two high tides per day?
a: Earth rotates through two tidal bulges daily (one on each side facing Moon).
why: Moon stays roughly over same latitude; as Earth spins, any coastal point passes through two bulges every 24+ hours.

q: What is a spring tide and what causes it?
a: Highest high tide; occurs when Sun and Moon align (new/full moon).
why: Sun and Moon gravity pull together, creating maximum tidal force and greatest water displacement.

q: What is a neap tide and when does it occur?
a: Lowest high tide; occurs when Sun and Moon are at right angles (quarter moons).
why: Sun and Moon gravity partially cancel, creating minimum tidal force and reduced water displacement.

q: What are ocean zones and name three based on light.
a: Epipelagic (sunlit), mesopelagic (twilight), bathypelagic (dark).
why: Light determines which photosynthetic organisms survive; depth/darkness creates distinct ecosystems with adapted species.

q: What is the thermocline and why is it important?
a: Rapid temperature transition layer in ocean; divides warm surface from cold deep water.
why: Thermocline acts as barrier to mixing; prevents nutrients from rising easily, limiting surface productivity.

q: What is upwelling and where does it occur?
a: Cold, nutrient-rich deep water rises to surface; occurs on western coasts under Ekman transport.
why: Upwelling brings nutrients that fuel phytoplankton growth, supporting fisheries—explains why coastlines are biologically productive.

q: What causes ocean acidification?
a: Atmospheric CO2 dissolves in seawater, forming carbonic acid, lowering pH.
why: Lower pH makes it harder for shells/skeletons to form, threatening corals, mollusks, and plankton.

q: How does continental drift provide evidence for plate tectonics?
a: Continents fit together (e.g., Africa-South America); fossils, rocks match across oceans.
why: Continents drifted apart on plates; fossil and rock similarities show they were once joined, confirming plate motion.

q: What is paleomagnetic evidence and how does it support plate tectonics?
a: Magnetic stripes on seafloor record reversals; symmetry shows spreading.
why: Ocean floor spreads as new basalt erupts; it records Earth's magnetic field reversals, proving seafloor is new and moving.

q: What is the age of oceanic crust and how does it vary?
a: Young at mid-ocean ridges (0 Ma); old at trenches (180+ Ma).
why: Crust forms at ridges and is recycled at subduction zones; distance from ridge determines age.

q: What is isostasy and how does it affect mountain stability?
a: Principle that lighter crust floats on denser mantle; thicker crust has deeper roots.
why: High mountains have deep roots of light crust; when eroded, crust rises to maintain equilibrium.

q: What is a hotspot and name one example.
a: Stationary plume of hot mantle rising through plate; example: Hawaii.
why: Hotspot stays fixed while plate moves over it, creating chain of volcanoes with age increasing in direction of plate motion.

q: How does the Hawaiian-Emperor chain provide evidence for plate motion?
a: Volcanoes get older along the chain, tracking the Pacific plate's past motion.
why: Volcanoes form when over hotspot, cool as plate carries them away, then new volcano forms over hotspot—creating age progression.

q: What is glaciation and what are ice ages?
a: Widespread glacial coverage; ice ages are periods when glaciers expand (100 ka cycles).
why: Milankovitch cycles (orbital changes) alter solar radiation reaching Earth; small changes accumulate, triggering ice advance/retreat.

q: What is the Snowball Earth hypothesis?
a: Theory that Earth was entirely ice-covered 600+ million years ago.
why: If true, extreme glaciation would reflect most sunlight, creating positive feedback; recovery required greenhouse gases to accumulate.

q: What are Milankovitch cycles and how do they affect climate?
a: Orbital variations (eccentricity, obliquity, precession) change solar radiation reaching Earth.
why: Cycles span 20-100 ka; small radiation changes trigger ice sheet feedbacks that amplify climate oscillations.

q: What is a glacial period and what caused the last glacial maximum?
a: Ice sheets covered vast areas; sea level was about 120 m lower.
why: Lower CO2 (~180 ppm) and Milankovitch cycles combined to cool Northern Hemisphere, expanding ice sheets.

q: What evidence shows past climate change?
a: Ice cores (CO2, isotopes), tree rings (precipitation), sediment layers (temperature proxies).
why: These records preserve climate signals: trapped air bubbles show past atmosphere, isotope ratios record temperature.

q: What is the difference between weather prediction and climate prediction?
a: Weather: days ahead (chaotic); climate: decades+ (predictable average).
why: Weather is sensitive to tiny initial differences (chaos); climate depends on energy balance (stable).

q: How much has global temperature increased since pre-industrial times?
a: ~1.1-1.2°C warming observed; ~0.2°C per decade recent trend.
why: Warming is unequivocal and accelerating; ocean absorption of heat has slowed atmospheric warming compared to models.

q: What are the main human sources of greenhouse gases?
a: Fossil fuel burning (CO2), agriculture/livestock (methane), industrial processes (N2O).
why: These sources add greenhouse gases faster than natural removal; accumulation drives radiative forcing and warming.

q: What is radiative forcing and how does it relate to warming?
a: Imbalance in energy in/out of Earth; positive forcing means net energy gain.
why: Each W/m² forcing causes equilibrium temperature rise; CO2 doubling is ~3.7 W/m² forcing.

q: What are climate tipping points and name one example.
a: Thresholds beyond which systems flip to new state; example: Amazon rainforest to savanna.
why: Feedback loops amplify change: warming reduces ice/snow reflectivity, exposing dark ground that absorbs more heat.

q: How does sea level rise from climate warming?
a: Two mechanisms: thermal expansion of water + melting of ice sheets/glaciers.
why: Thermal expansion accounts for ~50%; ice melt ~50%; both are accelerating as warming continues.

q: What is permafrost and why is it important to climate?
a: Frozen ground that stays below 0°C; contains large carbon stores (organic matter).
why: Permafrost thaw releases methane and CO2, amplifying warming—a dangerous positive feedback.

q: What is ocean stratification and how does it affect marine ecosystems?
a: Density layers prevent vertical mixing; reduces nutrient upwelling.
why: Warming increases stratification, trapping nutrients in deep water and reducing surface productivity.

q: What is a dust storm and what environmental conditions create it?
a: Massive dust cloud from arid regions; needs strong wind, dry soil, sparse vegetation.
why: Drought reduces vegetation; wind lifts soil particles; particles can travel thousands of km, affecting air quality globally.

q: What is soil and what are three main components?
a: Mixture of mineral particles, organic matter, water, air.
why: Soil forms slowly (centuries per cm) from weathered rock + decaying organisms; different ratios create different soil types.

q: What is soil pH and how does it affect plant growth?
a: Measure of acidity/alkalinity (0-14 scale); most plants prefer 6-7.
why: pH alters nutrient solubility; too acidic/alkaline locks up essential nutrients even if present.

q: What is the difference between weathering and soil formation?
a: Weathering breaks down rocks; soil formation adds organic matter and biological activity.
why: Soil is engineered by organisms and chemistry; pure weathered rock fragments are not soil.

q: What is karst topography and how does it form?
a: Landscape with sinkholes, caves, disappearing streams; forms in soluble rock (limestone).
why: Slightly acidic water dissolves limestone (CaCO3), creating voids and caves; collapse forms sinkholes.

q: What is a cave and what role do stalactites/stalagmites play?
a: Underground cavity; stalactites hang from ceiling, stalagmites build from floor (calcite deposits).
why: Water drips from ceiling, depositing calcite layer by layer over thousands of years.

q: What is a canyon and how are they formed?
a: Deep valley with steep walls; usually carved by rivers over millions of years.
why: River erosion removes rock faster than surrounding weathering; if rock is resistant, steep canyon walls form.

q: What is a delta and how does it form?
a: Triangular land mass where river meets ocean; sediment deposited as river slows.
why: River velocity drops at mouth; sediment settles, building land seaward until river cuts new course.

q: What is loess and where is it found?
a: Fine silt deposited by wind; found downwind of glaciers, deserts.
why: Wind carries finest particles far from source; when wind slows, silt settles as thick, uniform blanket.

q: What is a floodplain and why are they fertile?
a: Flat land beside river that floods; sediment deposits enrich soil.
why: Periodic floods carry nutrient-rich sediment; this natural fertilization made river valleys prime settlement sites.

q: What is groundwater and how does it move?
a: Water stored in soil/rock layers; moves slowly through pores under hydraulic gradient.
why: Gravity and pressure differences drive flow; residence time is centuries to millennia, much slower than surface flow.

q: What is an aquifer and why are they important?
a: Rock layer holding groundwater; supplies 2 billion people with drinking water.
why: Aquifers are renewable only if recharge exceeds extraction; many are being drained faster than refilled.

q: What is porosity and permeability in rocks?
a: Porosity: fraction of void space; permeability: ease of fluid flow through pores.
why: Gravel has high porosity/permeability (good aquifer); clay has high porosity but low permeability (poor aquifer).

q: What is saltwater intrusion and where does it occur?
a: Seawater replacing freshwater in aquifers near coasts due to overpumping.
why: Freshwater is less dense and floats on salt water; pumping removes freshwater, allowing salt water to advance.

q: What is the biome and name three main terrestrial biomes.
a: Large region with distinct climate, flora, fauna; examples: tropical rainforest, tundra, desert.
why: Biomes reflect climate patterns; tropical areas are warm/wet; poles are cold/dry; altitude creates similar zoning.

q: What is biodiversity and why does it decline?
a: Variety of species in ecosystem; declines from habitat loss, pollution, climate change.
why: When habitat shrinks, populations fall below sustainable size; smaller populations have less genetic diversity, increasing extinction risk.

q: What is the nitrogen cycle and why is it important?
a: Cycling of N through atmosphere, soil, organisms; essential for life.
why: Nitrogen is building block of proteins/DNA; cycle returns it to usable form via bacterial fixation.

q: What is the carbon cycle and how does human activity affect it?
a: Cycling of carbon through atmosphere, biota, oceans, sediments; humans add CO2 faster than natural removal.
why: Fossil fuel burning adds carbon from long-buried deposits; rate exceeds natural absorption by soils/oceans.

q: Name the four chambers of the heart.
a: Right atrium, right ventricle, left atrium, left ventricle.
why: The two atria receive blood, while the two ventricles pump it out; the right side handles pulmonary circulation and the left handles systemic circulation.

q: What is the primary function of hemoglobin?
a: Bind and transport oxygen from lungs to tissues.
why: Hemoglobin's iron-containing heme groups reversibly bind O2, enabling red blood cells to deliver oxygen to cells throughout the body.

q: Which organ produces insulin?
a: Pancreas, specifically the beta cells in the islets of Langerhans.
why: The pancreas acts as both an endocrine and exocrine gland; the islets secrete hormones including insulin to regulate blood glucose.

q: What are the three main types of blood vessels?
a: Arteries, capillaries, veins.
why: Arteries carry blood away from the heart under high pressure, capillaries enable exchange, and veins return blood to the heart.

q: Name the five lobes of the brain.
a: Frontal, parietal, temporal, occipital, insula.
why: The first four form the main cerebral cortex; the insula is deeper inside and involved in emotion, pain, and homeostasis regulation.

q: What is the function of the esophagus?
a: Transport food from mouth to stomach via peristalsis.
why: Muscular contractions propel food downward; the esophagus has no digestive role, only mechanical transit.

q: Define the autonomic nervous system and its two branches.
a: Controls involuntary functions; sympathetic (fight-or-flight) and parasympathetic (rest-and-digest).
why: The autonomic nervous system automatically regulates heart rate, digestion, and other vital functions without conscious effort.

q: What enzyme in saliva begins carbohydrate digestion?
a: Amylase.
why: Salivary amylase breaks down starch into smaller sugars; digestion starts in the mouth, not the stomach.

q: Which bone is the largest and strongest in the human body?
a: Femur (thighbone).
why: The femur bears most body weight and experiences the greatest forces during movement and support.

q: What is the role of the trachea?
a: Conduct air from larynx to lungs via branching bronchi.
why: The trachea is a rigid tube reinforced with cartilage to keep airways open; it splits into left and right main bronchi.

q: Name the three types of muscle tissue.
a: Skeletal, cardiac, smooth.
why: Skeletal muscles enable voluntary movement; cardiac muscle is found only in the heart; smooth muscle surrounds internal organs.

q: What do white blood cells (leukocytes) primarily do?
a: Defend against pathogens and foreign substances.
why: Various types of white blood cells patrol the body, engulfing bacteria and viruses or producing antibodies.

q: Which hormone regulates calcium levels in blood?
a: Parathyroid hormone (PTH) and calcitonin.
why: PTH raises calcium by releasing it from bone and kidneys; calcitonin lowers it by promoting bone deposition.

q: What is the function of the kidney's nephrons?
a: Filter waste from blood and produce urine.
why: Each kidney contains about one million nephrons that filter water and waste while reabsorbing useful substances.

q: Define synapses and their role in the nervous system.
a: Junctions between neurons where neurotransmitters transmit signals.
why: Synapses enable communication between neurons; neurotransmitters carry signals across the synaptic gap.

q: What is the primary role of the liver?
a: Filter blood, produce bile, metabolize drugs and toxins.
why: The liver detoxifies substances, produces glucose, breaks down hemoglobin, and synthesizes clotting factors.

q: How many chromosomes do most human cells contain?
a: 46 (23 pairs).
why: Gametes (sperm and egg) have 23; when they fuse at fertilization, the zygote gets the full 46.

q: What is photoreception and where does it occur?
a: Light detection by the eye; occurs in the retina's rod and cone cells.
why: Rod cells detect light intensity; cone cells detect color; signals travel to the optic nerve then brain.

q: Define homeostasis.
a: Maintenance of stable internal environment despite external changes.
why: Cells require constant temperature, pH, and osmotic pressure; organs work together to keep conditions steady.

q: What is peristalsis?
a: Rhythmic muscular contractions that move food through digestive tract.
why: Peristalsis is involuntary and works even if you're upside down; it's coordinated by the autonomic nervous system.

q: Name the three smallest bones in the human body.
a: Malleus, incus, stapes (ear ossicles).
why: These three bones in the middle ear amplify sound vibrations from the eardrum to the inner ear.

q: What is the function of antibodies?
a: Bind to antigens on pathogens to mark them for destruction.
why: Antibodies are proteins made by B cells; they enhance immune response by neutralizing toxins or flagging invaders.

q: Define the blood-brain barrier.
a: Semi-permeable membrane that restricts passage of substances from blood to brain.
why: Tight junctions between brain capillaries protect the brain from pathogens and toxins while allowing essential nutrients through.

q: What is the function of the corpus callosum?
a: Connect the two cerebral hemispheres and enable interhemispheric communication.
why: The corpus callosum contains millions of axons relaying information between left and right hemispheres.

q: Which organ breaks down fats into fatty acids?
a: Liver (produces bile) and pancreas (produces lipase).
why: Bile from the liver emulsifies fat; pancreatic lipase then chemically breaks down fats in the small intestine.

q: What is the ABO blood type system based on?
a: Presence or absence of A and B antigens on red blood cells.
why: Type A has A antigens, Type B has B antigens, Type AB has both, Type O has neither.

q: Name the four types of teeth and their primary functions.
a: Incisors (cut), canines (tear), premolars (grind), molars (grind).
why: Each tooth type is shaped for its function; adults have 32 teeth arranged for efficient food processing.

q: What is the role of thyroid hormone (T3 and T4)?
a: Regulate metabolic rate and energy production.
why: Thyroid hormone increases oxygen consumption and heat production in cells; deficiency causes fatigue and weight gain.

q: Define the Rh factor in blood.
a: Presence or absence of the D antigen on red blood cells.
why: Rh+ blood has the D antigen; Rh- lacks it; mismatched transfusions or pregnancies can cause hemolysis.

q: What is the function of cilia in the respiratory tract?
a: Sweep mucus and trapped particles upward toward the mouth.
why: Ciliary motion is coordinated; they remove pathogens and debris from the lungs and prevent infection.

q: What are the three regions of the small intestine?
a: Duodenum, jejunum, ileum.
why: The duodenum receives chyme from stomach and enzymes from pancreas; jejunum and ileum absorb nutrients.

q: Define mitochondria's role in the cell.
a: Generate ATP through cellular respiration to power cellular activities.
why: Mitochondria oxidize glucose and fatty acids, extracting energy in the form of ATP.

q: What is the function of the Eustachian tube?
a: Equalize air pressure between middle ear and atmosphere.
why: When you swallow or yawn, the Eustachian tube opens, preventing pressure build-up that would dampen hearing.

q: Name the three layers of the skin.
a: Epidermis, dermis, hypodermis (subcutaneous).
why: Epidermis is the outer barrier; dermis contains blood vessels and nerves; hypodermis is insulating fat layer.

q: What is the function of the pituitary gland?
a: Secrete hormones controlling growth, reproduction, and other endocrine glands.
why: The pituitary is the 'master gland'; anterior lobe makes growth hormone and gonadotropins; posterior releases ADH and oxytocin.

q: Define the reflex arc and its purpose.
a: Sensory neuron → spinal cord → motor neuron pathway allowing rapid involuntary responses.
why: Reflexes bypass the brain for speed; pulling hand from hot surface happens before pain signal reaches conscious awareness.

q: What is the function of gastric juice in the stomach?
a: Digest proteins and kill pathogens; hydrochloric acid and pepsin are key components.
why: HCl denatures proteins and activates pepsin; the acidic environment destroys most bacteria.

q: Name the chambers of the cochlea in the inner ear.
a: Scala vestibuli, scala tympani, scala media.
why: Sound vibrations move fluid in these chambers; hair cells in scala media detect vibrations and send signals to the brain.

q: What is the primary function of cholesterol in the body?
a: Build cell membranes and synthesize steroid hormones and vitamin D.
why: Cholesterol is essential; the body makes most of it; only excess intake elevates blood cholesterol dangerously.

q: Define the glomerulus and its role in filtration.
a: Network of capillaries in nephron that filters blood to form filtrate.
why: High pressure forces water, glucose, ions, and urea into Bowman's capsule; large proteins and cells stay in blood.

q: What is the function of surfactant in the lungs?
a: Reduce surface tension of lung fluid to prevent alveoli collapse.
why: Without surfactant, alveoli would stick together and not inflate; premature infants lack it causing respiratory distress.

q: Name the six primary taste sensations.
a: Sweet, salty, sour, bitter, umami, fat.
why: Taste buds contain receptors for these flavors; umami (savory) and fat taste were recently confirmed as distinct sensations.

q: What is the function of the duodenum?
a: First part of small intestine; neutralize stomach acid and receive pancreatic enzymes and bile.
why: The duodenum's slightly basic pH and digestive secretions are essential for breaking down all major nutrients.

q: Define the term 'gene' and how traits are inherited.
a: A segment of DNA coding for a protein; traits follow Mendelian patterns through alleles.
why: Dominant alleles mask recessive ones in heterozygotes; Punnett squares predict offspring traits from parental genotypes.

q: What role does the hypothalamus play in temperature regulation?
a: Monitor body temperature and trigger sweating or shivering to maintain set point.
why: The hypothalamus acts as the body's thermostat, setting temperature at 37°C and correcting deviations.

q: What is the function of prolactin?
a: Stimulate milk production in mammary glands after birth.
why: Prolactin levels rise during pregnancy and lactation; suckling triggers releases that sustain milk supply.

q: Define osteocytes and their role in bone.
a: Mature bone cells that maintain bone matrix and respond to mechanical stress.
why: Osteocytes occupy lacunae in bone and communicate to detect stress, signaling osteoblasts to strengthen bone.

q: What is the function of the gallbladder?
a: Store and concentrate bile to aid fat digestion.
why: Bile breaks down fats into smaller droplets; the gallbladder releases it when fatty food enters small intestine.

q: Name the five lobes of the lungs.
a: Right upper, right middle, right lower, left upper, left lower.
why: The right lung has three lobes; the left lung has two lobes (smaller to accommodate the heart).

q: What is the purpose of the semicircular canals in the ear?
a: Detect rotational movements and sense balance and equilibrium.
why: Fluid in semicircular canals bends hair cells when you turn your head; signals go to the cerebellum.

q: Define crossing over and its significance in meiosis.
a: Exchange of genetic material between homologous chromosomes, creating genetic variation.
why: Crossing over shuffles alleles; combined with independent assortment, it produces genetic diversity in offspring.

q: What is the function of histamine in immune response?
a: Trigger inflammation, vasodilation, and increased vascular permeability.
why: Mast cells and basophils release histamine; it causes the redness, swelling, and itching of allergic reactions.

q: Name the four parathyroid glands' primary hormone.
a: Parathyroid hormone (PTH).
why: PTH increases blood calcium by releasing it from bone, increasing reabsorption in kidneys, and activating vitamin D.

q: What is the function of tight junctions in epithelial tissue?
a: Prevent passage of substances between cells; force movement through cells.
why: Tight junctions seal cell-to-cell gaps; they maintain barriers like the blood-brain barrier and intestinal epithelium.

q: Define the role of myelin in neuronal signaling.
a: Insulate axons to speed up action potential propagation.
why: Myelin sheaths around axons enable saltatory conduction; unmyelinated axons conduct signals much more slowly.

q: What is the function of the pancreatic islets (islets of Langerhans)?
a: Secrete insulin, glucagon, and somatostatin to regulate glucose.
why: Beta cells make insulin (lowers glucose), alpha cells make glucagon (raises glucose), delta cells make somatostatin.

q: Name the three main types of sensory receptors.
a: Mechanoreceptors, chemoreceptors, photoreceptors.
why: Mechanoreceptors detect touch and pressure; chemoreceptors sense chemicals; photoreceptors detect light.

q: What is the function of the ciliary body in the eye?
a: Adjust lens thickness to focus light on the retina.
why: Ciliary muscles contract to relax lens fibers, allowing the lens to bulge for near vision (accommodation).

q: Define phenotype and genotype.
a: Phenotype is observable traits; genotype is genetic makeup (alleles present).
why: Environmental factors affect phenotype; genotype is fixed unless mutation occurs, determining potential phenotypes.

q: What is the function of the zona pellucida?
a: Protective transparent layer around egg; prevents polyspermy.
why: Zona pellucida hardens after one sperm fertilizes the egg, blocking additional sperm from entering.

q: What do neutrophils do in the immune response?
a: First responders that engulf and destroy bacteria via phagocytosis.
why: Neutrophils are the most abundant white blood cell; they arrive first at infection sites and die en masse forming pus.

q: Define the role of glucagon in blood glucose regulation.
a: Raise blood glucose by stimulating glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis.
why: When blood glucose drops, pancreatic alpha cells release glucagon; it mobilizes stored glucose from the liver.

q: What is the function of the lens in the eye?
a: Refract light and focus it on the retina.
why: The lens is biconvex and flexible; its shape changes to bend light precisely onto the fovea.

q: Name the three functional regions of a neuron.
a: Dendrite (receives), soma (integrates), axon (transmits).
why: Dendrites collect signals; the soma sums them; the axon fires if threshold is reached and sends signal onward.

q: What is the function of the prostate gland?
a: Secrete fluid that protects and nourishes sperm in semen.
why: Prostate fluid is alkaline, protecting sperm from vaginal acidity; it comprises 30% of semen volume.

q: Define the term 'allele' and provide an example.
a: Alternative forms of a gene; example: alleles for pea plant height (tall or short).
why: Alleles result from mutations; they occupy the same locus on homologous chromosomes and code for different trait versions.

q: What is the function of the diaphragm in respiration?
a: Primary muscle for breathing; contracts to increase thoracic volume and inflate lungs.
why: Diaphragm movement accounts for 70% of breathing; when it relaxes, lungs deflate passively.

q: Name the structure that connects fetus to placenta.
a: Umbilical cord.
why: The umbilical cord contains two arteries (carrying deoxygenated blood) and one vein (carrying oxygenated blood to fetus).

q: What is the function of memory cells in the immune system?
a: Persist long-term and provide rapid response upon re-exposure to antigen.
why: Memory cells enable vaccine protection; they respond faster and stronger on second exposure (secondary immune response).

q: Define the role of acetylcholine at the neuromuscular junction.
a: Neurotransmitter that triggers skeletal muscle contraction.
why: Acetylcholine binds to receptors on muscle fiber, depolarizing it and triggering action potential and contraction.

q: What is the function of the appendix?
a: Vestigial organ; likely aids immune function by housing lymphoid tissue.
why: The appendix contains gut-associated lymphoid tissue; its role is minimal in modern humans but inflammation causes appendicitis.

q: Name the primary hormone of the adrenal medulla.
a: Epinephrine (adrenaline).
why: Epinephrine triggers fight-or-flight responses: increased heart rate, blood pressure, and glucose mobilization.

q: What is the function of the placenta during pregnancy?
a: Exchange nutrients, gases, and wastes between fetus and mother; produce hormones.
why: Placenta is not a barrier; it enables diffusion of oxygen and glucose to fetus while removing CO2 and urea.

q: Define genotype AA, Aa, and aa.
a: AA homozygous dominant, Aa heterozygous, aa homozygous recessive.
why: Heterozygotes show dominant phenotype if inheritance is simple dominant; homozygous recessive always shows recessive phenotype.

q: What is the function of microvilli in the small intestine?
a: Increase surface area for nutrient absorption.
why: Microvilli multiply absorptive surface area 600-fold; each villus contains capillaries and lacteals for transport.

q: Name the three types of cone cells and their wavelengths.
a: S (blue, 420nm), M (green, 530nm), L (red, 560nm).
why: Cone cells contain different opsins sensitive to short, medium, long wavelengths; their combined signals create color vision.

q: What is the function of the tonsils and adenoids?
a: Filter pathogens from throat; contain lymphoid tissue for immune response.
why: Tonsils are enlarged during infection; removing them doesn't harm immunity since other lymphoid tissues compensate.

q: Define the role of hemoglobin F in fetal development.
a: Fetal hemoglobin with higher oxygen affinity than adult hemoglobin.
why: Higher affinity allows fetal blood to extract oxygen from maternal blood across placenta; replaced by hemoglobin A after birth.

q: What is the function of the loop of Henle in nephron filtration?
a: Create osmotic gradient to allow water reabsorption and urine concentration.
why: Descending limb is permeable to water, ascending limb is not; this multiplier effect concentrates urine in medulla.

q: Name the four types of taste receptor cells.
a: Type I, Type II, Type III, Type IV.
why: Type II cells sense sweet, umami, bitter; Type III sense sour; Type IV are involved in taste signaling.

q: What is the function of the fovea centralis?
a: Region of sharpest vision on retina; contains only cone cells.
why: The fovea has highest cone density and provides detailed central vision; peripheral retina has more rods for dim light.

q: Define the role of corticotropin-releasing hormone (CRH).
a: Signal anterior pituitary to release ACTH, stimulating cortisol release.
why: CRH is released by hypothalamus during stress; cortisol increases blood glucose and suppresses immune response.

q: What is the function of intercalated cells in kidney collecting duct?
a: Regulate acid-base balance by secreting H+ or HCO3- ions.
why: Type A cells secrete acid to lower pH; Type B cells secrete bicarbonate to raise pH based on body needs.

q: Name the three types of fibrous proteins in bone matrix.
a: Type I collagen, elastin, proteoglycans.
why: Collagen provides tensile strength; proteoglycans bind water; together they make bone flexible and strong.

q: What is the function of the vestibular system in balance?
a: Detect head position and acceleration; coordinate posture and eye movements.
why: Vestibular nuclei in brainstem integrate signals from utricle, saccule, and semicircular canals to maintain balance.

q: Define the term 'dominant' and 'recessive' alleles.
a: Dominant alleles mask recessive alleles in heterozygotes; recessive only show in homozygotes.
why: Dominance is a pattern of inheritance; one allele produces enough protein to mask the other's effect.

q: What is the function of the cribriform plate?
a: Bone plate allowing olfactory nerve axons to pass from nose to olfactory bulb.
why: Smell signals bypass thalamus; olfactory bulb connects directly to limbic system, explaining smell's emotional power.

q: Name the five types of white blood cells.
a: Neutrophils, eosinophils, basophils, lymphocytes, monocytes.
why: Neutrophils fight bacteria; eosinophils target parasites; lymphocytes are B and T cells; monocytes become macrophages.

q: What is the function of the brush border on intestinal epithelium?
a: Increase surface area and contain enzymes for final nutrient breakdown.
why: Brush border forms from microvilli; it contains disaccharidases and peptidases that complete carbohydrate and protein digestion.

q: Define the role of the basal ganglia in motor control.
a: Select and initiate voluntary movements; inhibit unwanted competing movements.
why: Dopamine-containing neurons in basal ganglia are damaged in Parkinson's disease, causing tremor and rigidity.

q: What is the primary difference between a diesel engine and a petrol engine?
a: Diesel uses compression ignition; petrol uses spark plugs.
why: Diesel's higher compression ratio ignites fuel through heat alone, making it more efficient; petrol requires electrical spark.

q: What does the acronym HVAC stand for?
a: Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning.
why: HVAC systems manage indoor climate by controlling temperature, airflow, and humidity in buildings.

q: How do solar panels generate electricity?
a: Photons strike silicon, freeing electrons to flow as electric current.
why: The photoelectric effect converts light energy directly to electrical energy without moving parts.

q: What is the primary function of a transformer in electrical systems?
a: Convert alternating current voltage between different levels.
why: Transformers step voltage up for transmission (reduce losses) or down for use by lowering current.

q: What is tensile strength in material science?
a: Maximum stress a material can withstand while being stretched before breaking.
why: Engineers use tensile strength to ensure cables, beams, and bridges won't snap under load.

q: Why are suspension bridges hung from cables rather than supported by columns throughout?
a: Cables efficiently distribute weight over longer spans with less material.
why: This allows bridges to span much greater distances than beam bridges while using less steel.

q: What is an internal combustion engine?
a: Engine that burns fuel inside a chamber to create expanding gases for motion.
why: Explosions directly push pistons; external combustion engines (like steam) burn fuel outside the cylinder.

q: How does a wind turbine convert wind to electricity?
a: Wind spins rotor blades that turn a generator shaft producing electricity.
why: Kinetic energy from wind drives mechanical rotation, and generators convert that to electrical current.

q: What is the Bessemer process?
a: Industrial process that mass-produces steel by removing carbon from cast iron.
why: Revolutionized steel production in the 1850s by making strong, cheap steel available for bridges and railways.

q: Why do airplane wings have a curved top and flatter bottom?
a: Curved top creates lower pressure, flat bottom higher pressure, generating upward lift.
why: This airfoil shape makes air move faster over top, reducing pressure via Bernoulli's principle.

q: What is a steam engine?
a: Engine that converts steam pressure into mechanical motion using pistons and cylinders.
why: Burning fuel heats water; expanding steam pushes pistons—the engine that powered the Industrial Revolution.

q: What does a transformer do in the power grid?
a: Changes AC voltage levels between circuits via electromagnetic induction.
why: High-voltage transmission cuts resistive losses; transformers step voltage down for safe home use.

q: What is reinforced concrete and why is it used?
a: Concrete with embedded steel bars for tensile strength it normally lacks.
why: Concrete resists compression, steel resists tension—combining them creates strong structures.

q: How does a hydroelectric power plant generate electricity?
a: Water flows through turbines connected to generators producing electrical current.
why: Gravitational potential energy converts to kinetic energy spinning the generator rotor.

q: What is the four-stroke cycle in an engine?
a: Intake, compression, combustion/power, and exhaust strokes.
why: Each stroke completes part of the burn cycle; four strokes complete one full cycle per two crankshaft rotations.

q: Who invented the light bulb and in what year?
a: Thomas Edison in 1879 developed the first practical incandescent bulb.
why: Edison's carbon filament bulb burned for hours, making electric lighting practical for homes and streets.

q: What is a differential gear in a car?
a: Device allowing rear wheels to rotate at different speeds while turning.
why: Inside wheels travel shorter distance than outside wheels in turns; differential prevents tire slip and binding.

q: What is the difference between series and parallel circuits?
a: Series: components in one loop; parallel: components in separate branches.
why: Series adds voltages but current is shared; parallel keeps voltage same but divides current across branches.

q: How does a catapult use physics to launch projectiles?
a: Stored mechanical energy in twisted rope or bent wood releases suddenly as kinetic energy.
why: Lever arms and mechanical advantage multiply force, accelerating the projectile quickly.

q: What is the purpose of ball bearings in machinery?
a: Reduce friction between rotating and stationary parts using rolling motion.
why: Rolling friction is much lower than sliding friction, reducing energy loss and extending component life.

q: Who developed the first practical AC induction motor?
a: Nikola Tesla developed the first practical AC induction motor.
why: Tesla's AC motor was simpler and more reliable than DC motors, powering the modern industrial age.

q: What is a hydraulic system?
a: System using pressurized fluid to transmit and control force through tubes and pistons.
why: Fluids are incompressible, allowing small movements to produce large forces with precise control.

q: What is the difference between renewable and nonrenewable energy sources?
a: Renewable regenerates naturally; nonrenewable depletes over time.
why: Renewables (sun, wind, water) are sustainable indefinitely; nonrenewables (oil, coal, gas) have finite reserves.

q: How does a railroad track stay level across uneven ground?
a: Subgrade is graded and leveled; ballast (stones) redistributes weight to support rails.
why: This distributes train weight across wide area, preventing rails from sinking into soil.

q: What is a gear ratio and what does it do?
a: Ratio of teeth on driven gear to driver gear; determines speed and torque multiplication.
why: Higher ratios provide more torque at lower speed; lower ratios allow higher speed with less torque.

q: Who invented the telephone and when?
a: Alexander Graham Bell patented it in 1876.
why: Bell's telephone converted sound waves to electrical signals and back, enabling voice transmission over wires.

q: What is a bearing and what types exist?
a: Device supporting a rotating shaft; types include ball, roller, and plain bearings.
why: Each type suits different loads and speeds—ball bearings for speed, roller for heavy loads.

q: How does a nuclear power plant generate electricity?
a: Nuclear fission heats water to steam, spinning turbines connected to generators.
why: Splitting uranium atoms releases energy efficiently, producing steam like coal plants but without combustion.

q: What is the purpose of a flywheel in engines?
a: Smooth and regulate rotation speed by storing and releasing rotational energy.
why: Heavy disk resists speed changes, preventing jerky power delivery and maintaining momentum between ignitions.

q: What is a pneumatic system?
a: System using compressed air to transmit and control force through tubes and pistons.
why: Air is compressible but simple and cheap; used in factories, tools, and automation.

q: Who invented the steam locomotive and when?
a: George Stephenson built the Rocket in 1829.
why: The Rocket proved steam locomotives were practical, launching railway transportation across industrial nations.

q: What is thermal expansion in materials?
a: Materials expand when heated and contract when cooled.
why: Engineers must account for expansion gaps in bridges and railways or they buckle in summer heat.

q: How does a pulley system reduce effort?
a: Multiple pulleys increase mechanical advantage by distributing load across more rope.
why: Load is divided among rope segments; pulling with less force lifts heavy objects.

q: What is an inclined plane and why is it useful?
